retrieved_docs.title: Heroin Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Heroin is one of the most commonly used drugs among intravenous drug abusers. Conventionally, chemical compounds that are fractionated from the juice of the opium poppy (Papaver somniferum) are known as opiates. Similarly, acting synthetic chemical compounds are known as opioids. The predominant opiate is found in opium poppy juice. The juice of the opium poppy is harvested, which contains a variety of opiates, mostly morphine. Additional processing is necessary to refine the opium poppy liquid into heroin. Heroin is synthesized from morphine by acetylation at both 3 and 6 positions and metabolized in the human body to active opioid compounds first by deacetylation to 6 mono acetyl morphine (6MAM) and then by further deacetylation to morphine. Heroin is smaller per dose, making it the version of the product preferred by drug smugglers. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Heroin metabolizes in the CNS to monoacetylmorphine, which is a more potent mu-receptor agonist than morphine. [6] Peripheral tissues metabolize heroin to 6-monoacetylmorphine and then morphine. [1] Many state toxicology labs use the 6-monoacetylmorphine to determine which overdose deaths were due to heroin.

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Professionals thoroughly describe heroin toxicity in the literature and popular media. With the high rate of prescription opioid addiction (see opioid use disorder) and the lower cost of heroin, many individuals have developed heroin dependence. [15] As discussed above, heroin (especially if administered intravenously) is likely to cause an overdose and death due to respiratory depression. From 2002 through 2013, heroin overdoses went from 0.7 deaths per 100,000 to 2.7. [16] In 2013, roughly 517,000 people reported heroin use in the past year, 1.5 times the amount in 2007. [17] State and federal legislation are implementing diverse methods to curb this epidemic of heroin overdose deaths. The many infectious and economic effects of heroin dependence add to the risk of death.

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Heroin's chief place in American healthcare remains problematic. As an illicit opioid, people have misused heroin for decades. [4] In the 21st century, the use of heroin has caused a surge in both dependence and overdose deaths. [5] Though people in other countries use heroin, in the United States, it has no recognized therapeutic role in managing opioid addiction.

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: When taken orally, heroin undergoes first-pass metabolism to morphine via deacetylation. Therefore, unlike intravenous administration, oral ingestion does not cause a rapid onset of effects and is less desirable to users. 6-monoacetylmorphine and morphine activate opioid receptors. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the basic pharmacology of heroin in the human body. Review the signs and symptoms expected on examination and evaluation of a patient with suspected heroin toxicity. Outline the management options available for both acute heroin toxicity and long-term management following recovery from heroin toxicity. Explain possible interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the evaluation and treatment of heroin toxicity and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Again, with no approved use for heroin, no therapeutic index exists. However, pharmacodynamically, heroin could be considered to have a very narrow therapeutic window. Due to potency and concentration variations, users are unaware of the actual amount of the active drug in any given sample. [14] Therefore, the same apparent dose that causes euphoria one day could very well lead to an overdose the next. Drug levels are not typically useful, though toxicology assessments post-mortem may quantify several metabolites. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Heroin is synthetically derived from the morphine alkaloid in opium and is approximately twice as potent as morphine. Heroin acts agonistically on the central nervous system (CNS) opioid receptors mu, kappa, and delta. [1] Mu receptor effects account for both the analgesic effects (Mu1) and the respiratory depression and euphoria (Mu2). [6] Activation of Mu2 receptors also causes miosis, reduced gastrointestinal (GI) motility, and physiologic dependence. Kappa receptor activation causes some degree of analgesia as well. [6] Delta receptors are more involved in spinal analgesia phenomena.

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the heroin overdose setting, hypopnea (breathing ineffectively slow and/or shallow) can progress to apnea. Naloxone is a mainstay of therapy in this setting, but the practitioner is warned that first-line treatment is control of the airway and rescue breathing. Bag valve mask ventilation or similar intervention should be initiated immediately by the primary rescuer to restore oxygen supply to vital organs while other rescuers evaluate available methods of naloxone administration. A single rescuer should focus on providing oxygen to the overdose victim until other rescuers are available to assist. Basic Life Support and Advanced Cardiac Life Support principles should be followed during the resuscitation of a heroin overdose. [18] Adequate intravenous access is necessary so enough fluids and medication can be administered. An initial intravenous dose of 0.4 to 0.8  mg of naloxone will usually quickly reverse neurologic and cardiorespiratory symptoms if the overdose is pure heroin and not contaminated by carfentanyl and similar substances. [19] Frequent monitoring of vital signs and cardiorespiratory status is needed to make sure Heroin is cleared from the patient system. Patients treated with naloxone after heroin overdose may be safely released without transport to the hospital or emergency room if they have normal mentation and vital signs. In the absence of co-intoxicants and further opioid use, there is a very low risk of death from rebound heroin toxicity. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: The intended effects of heroin misuse are those classically associated with any opioid effects. These are analgesia, euphoria, and often alleviation of opioid withdrawal symptoms. All other effects of heroin could be considered adverse. Respiratory depression is likely the most concerning adverse effect, leading to death in an increasing number of misusers. Extreme physiologic dependence also represents a major concern in those who misuse heroin.

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most recent classification scheme identifies three major classes of opioid receptors, with several minor classes. The three most clinically relevant opioid receptors are the mu, kappa, and delta receptors. Stimulation of central mu receptors causes respiratory depression, analgesia (supraspinal and peripheral), and euphoria. Kappa and delta-opioid receptors also have potent analgesic effects, with the kappa receptors being known for causing disassociation, hallucinations, and dysphoria. Delta receptors also modulate mu receptors and are thought to influence mood. Heroin has effects on the opioid receptors, particularly the mu receptor. It also has effects on the kappa and delta receptors. There is an evolving body of knowledge that the intensity and quality of response to heroin and other opioids can vary significantly between patients, which can be unrelated to tolerance. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Intravenous drug use can lead to infectious complications discussed elsewhere (such as cellulitis, thrombophlebitis, endocarditis, septic emboli, and compartment syndrome, for example). Adulterants in heroin can be quite diverse and sometimes unknown to the consumer and can confound the evaluator by giving a potentially very broad spectrum of conflicting physical exam findings. Law enforcement may have knowledge of the current adulterant blend of the heroin that is being locally consumed at the time of the encounter. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin Toxicity -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Heroin use, abuse, overdose, and addiction have had many discussions in the professional and lay press of the United States, which intensified in the 2000s and 2010s. After around 2012, the United States government began to shift the prioritization of money from interdiction and law enforcement to treatment programs to treat drug addicts, including those addicted to heroin. [37] This focus shift was brought about by several factors. One factor was a growing recognition that interdiction efforts, known commonly as the drug war, were not yielding results equivalent to the effort and money invested. There was also growing professional and lay acceptance of the concept of medication-assisted treatment, involving replacing the heroin with another full agonist opioid such as methadone or partial opioid agonist like buprenorphine. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin (Archived) -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned above, practitioners in the United States do not administer heroin in any sanctioned healthcare setting. Illicitly, people misuse heroin via subcutaneous, intranasal, intramuscular, and intravenous methods. Heroin is highly lipophilic and, therefore, rapidly crosses the blood-brain barrier. [1] All routes of administration lead to the rapid absorption of heroin. Peak serum levels in these routes are 5 to 10 minutes subcutaneously, 3 to 5 minutes intranasally and intramuscularly, and less than one minute intravenously. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Heroin Toxicity -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: The action of heroin on the opioid receptors is fairly well characterized at this point. An ongoing area of investigation is forensic, namely what opioid contaminants might be lurking within the next shipment of heroin coming into the United States from elsewhere. Many of these contaminants, such as carfentanyl, are not found on usual drug screens, and gas chromatography/mass spectroscopy can be needed to determine the exact contents of a given dose. [22] There is also growing interest in the potential for cannabis to displace opioids such as heroin as a drug of choice, which follows the harm reduction model, as cannabis is less lethal in the setting of overdose compared to heroin. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 92
retrieved_docs.content: the anticholinergic pharmacologic class. Collectively, these entities and relationships demonstrate that alcohol, cocaine, heroin, hallucinogens, opioids, and other psychoactive drugs are interlinked through shared diagnostic categories, dependence mechanisms, poisoning risks, and pharmacologic interventions, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and prevention of toxic complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: and poisoning as interrelated clinical entities; connects specific substance use disorders (alcohol, tobacco, cocaine, hallucinogens, heroin, stimulants, anabolic steroids, antidepressants) with their toxic, addictive, and physiological outcomes; and situates relevant pharmacologic treatments and regulatory categories within an integrated medical framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2252
retrieved_docs.content: Heroin is chemically derived from morphine, indicating that morphine serves as its parent compound. Additionally, heroin is not classified as a phytonutrient, distinguishing it from plant‑derived nutritional substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 190
retrieved_docs.content: is itself a child of تسمم بالهيروين (heroin poisoning) and a parent of سمية الكوكايين (cocaine toxicity), illustrating a nested structure among major opioid and stimulant poisonings. Heroin poisoning (تسمم بالهيروين) is a distinct Disorder caused by psychoactive substances and simultaneously a subclass of poisoning by analgesic drugs, poisoning caused by biological substances, and poisoning due to chemical substances, reflecting the multifactorial nature of opioid toxicity. It is linked to broader concepts of opioid intoxication, general toxicity, and to suicide or intentional self‑inflicted injury through its association with poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics. Organic mental disorders caused by psychoactive substances include Sedative, hypnotic, and/or anxiolytic intoxication delirium and hallucinogen intoxication, demonstrating the neuropsychiatric sequelae of exposure to sedatives, hypnotics, anxiolytics, and hallucinogens. SSRI withdrawal is a specific

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 253
retrieved_docs.content: concepts depict a comprehensive view of substance‑related disorders, encompassing diagnostic criteria (e.g., cocaine withdrawal), various substance‑induced psychiatric and behavioral states, hierarchical classifications across languages, specific syndromes such as drug withdrawal, therapeutic approaches like opioid maintenance, and physiological sequelae such as rebound tachycardia. This integrated framework aids clinicians in recognizing the full spectrum of substance‑related conditions, their diagnostic components, and associated treatment and complication pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 59
retrieved_docs.content: a comprehensive, hierarchical view of heroin‑related and broader toxic exposures, their connections to drug dependence, psychotropic poisoning, infectious and immune conditions, self‑inflicted injury, psychiatric disorders, and relevant therapeutic and safety considerations essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 889
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion for substance‑related disorders, indicating that the presence of withdrawal symptoms from cocaine contributes to the classification of these disorders. Substance‑related disorders are organized under the broader category of withdrawal and relapse effects, and they include the drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) as one of their members. The drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) also encompasses specific treatment approaches such as maintenance therapy for opioid substances, reflecting the role of opioid maintenance programs in managing withdrawal. Within the overarching framework of withdrawal and relapse effects, rebound tachycardia is identified as a specific physiological manifestation that can occur during withdrawal or after relapse. This interconnected structure highlights how withdrawal phenomena, diagnostic criteria, syndromic classifications, therapeutic strategies, and acute cardiovascular responses are linked in the clinical understanding and management of substance‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 357
retrieved_docs.content: Dimemorfan is classified as a morphinan opioid, indicating that it belongs to the morphinan opioid family of compounds. This relationship places dimemorfan within the broader class of opioids that share the morphinan chemical structure, which is relevant for understanding its pharmacological properties and potential clinical uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 949
retrieved_docs.content: Antidepressant drug adverse reaction is a type of disorder caused by psychoactive substance. The broader category disorder caused by psychoactive substance encompasses heroin poisoning (تسمم بالهيروين), which is classified under poisoning by other medications and drugs. Heroin poisoning is regarded both as poisoning caused by a biological substance and as poisoning due to a chemical substance, reflecting its dual nature as a biologically derived opioid with a distinct chemical composition. The classification poisoning by other medications and drugs also includes the late effect of poisoning due to drug, medicinal, or biological substance, indicating that sequelae of drug‑related poisonings are captured within the same group. Tryptophan poisoning is another example that belongs simultaneously to poisoning caused by a biological substance and to poisoning due to a chemical substance, illustrating overlapping categories within toxicology. This integrated hierarchy clarifies how various drug‑related adverse reactions and poisonings are related, aiding clinicians in accurate identification, categorization, and management of such conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 498
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by other medications and drugs serves as a broad category that includes heroin poisoning (تسمم بالهيروين) and also encompasses the late effect of poisoning due to drug, medicinal, or biological substances. Heroin poisoning is simultaneously regarded as a specific instance of poisoning caused by a biological substance and of poisoning due to a chemical substance, reflecting its dual nature as both a biologically‑derived and chemically‑processed opioid. Likewise, poisoning caused by a biological substance and poisoning due to a chemical substance each have Tryptophan poisoning as a subordinate type, indicating that Tryptophan toxicity can be classified under both biological and chemical poisoning frameworks. Together, these relationships illustrate that certain poisonings—such as heroin and Tryptophan poisoning—fit within multiple overlapping classification schemes, highlighting the complexity of categorizing toxic exposures for clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: buprenorphine is described both as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant‑derived agent product, while lofexidine and beta‑funaltrexamine function as narcotics antagonists. Together, these entities illustrate intersecting pharmacologic classifications across cardiovascular rhythm management, antihypertensive therapy, and opioid dependence treatment, providing clinicians with a structured understanding of drug classes, therapeutic indications, formulation options, and safety considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2048
retrieved_docs.content: 1‑Methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin, apomorphine, and dihydroxyfenylalanine are all classified as dopaminergic agents, a specific subgroup of neurotransmitter substances. As dopaminergic agents, they influence dopamine signaling and include mechanisms such as inhibitory dopamine reuptake (“inhibitory vychytávání dopaminu”). Apomorphine is chemically derived from morphine, indicating a parent‑child relationship between apomorphine and morphine, and it represents a narrower, more specialized category within the broader class of dopaminergic agents. Together, these compounds illustrate how individual molecules (1‑methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin, apomorphine, dihydroxyfenylalanine) fit into the hierarchical structure of neurotransmitter pharmacology, linking specific agents to their parent class (neurotransmitter substances) and to distinct functional subtypes (such as agents that inhibit dopamine reuptake). This hierarchical organization aids clinicians and researchers in understanding the pharmacodynamic properties and therapeutic potentials of dopaminergic drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2252
retrieved_docs.content: الاعتماد على الأدوية (drug dependence) serves as a broad categorical framework that unites several substance‑related conditions. إدمان الهيروين (heroin addiction) is classified explicitly as a form of الاعتماد على الأدوية, indicating that heroin use disorder falls within the general concept of drug dependence. Likewise, التسمم المرضي بالعقاقير (pathological drug poisoning) is identified as a child concept of الاعتماد على الأدوية, showing that drug‑induced poisoning is also encompassed by this overarching category. The topic Poisonings and Drug Effects is linked to الاعتماد على الأدوية as its parent, further connecting the study of drug effects and poisonings to the larger domain of drug dependence. This hierarchical organization underscores how heroin addiction, pathological drug poisoning, and the broader field of poisonings and drug effects are interrelated under the umbrella of drug dependence, which is essential for comprehensive clinical assessment, treatment planning, and public‑health interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 121
retrieved_docs.content: (تسمم بالهيروين). Heroin poisoning serves both as a specific manifestation within the larger domains of poisoning caused by biological substances and poisoning due to chemical substances, and as an anchor for related toxicities such as cocaine toxicity (سمية الكوكايين) and unspecified psychoactive substance poisoning (تسمم بمؤثرات عقلية غير محددة). Poisoning by analgesic drugs is a broader class that encompasses heroin poisoning and is linked to intentional self‑harm, reflecting its relevance to suicide and self‑inflicted injury. A more specific subclass, “Poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics,” is positioned as a child of heroin poisoning while also belonging to the general drug‑poisoning group (التسمم بالعقاقير). At the highest hierarchical level, POISONING BY DRUGS, MEDICINAL AND BIOLOGICAL SUBSTANCES is a child of the analgesics/antipyretics/antirheumatics category, which itself is a child of the overall drug‑poisoning category, illustrating the nested nature

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 91
retrieved_docs.content: heroin poisoning (تسمم بالهيروين); heroin poisoning is further classified under poisoning by analgesic drugs, poisoning by analgesics/antipyretics/antirheumatics, poisoning by other medications and drugs, poisoning by biological substances, and poisoning by chemical substances, reflecting its status as an opioid intoxication and a representative of general toxicity from diverse agents. Poisoning by analgesic drugs is explicitly associated with suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury, underscoring the clinical relevance of intentional overdose. Organic mental disorders caused by psychoactive substances include sedative, hypnotic, or anxiolytic intoxication delirium and hallucinogen intoxication, which are nested under the opioid‑intoxication branch, illustrating overlapping pathophysiology between substance‑induced mental disorders and opioid toxicity. Adverse events during medical or surgical treatment are captured as unspecified accidents during medical and surgical treatment,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1076
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in opioid dependence comprise buprenorfin, levomethadone and lofexidine. Buprenorfin is additionally classified as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant‑derived agent product, indicating its origin from botanical sources. Both lofexidine and beta‑funaltrexamine are identified as narcotic antagonists, reflecting their role in blocking opioid receptor activity. This collection of agents illustrates that medications for opioid dependence span several pharmacologic categories, including natural alkaloids, synthetic antagonists and other agents, and that recognizing these classifications is important for selecting appropriate treatment strategies in opioid use disorder.
